patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/8] nfsd: fixes for locally-triggerable bugs

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/8] nfsd: defer vfree of compound ops to fix rpc_status UAF
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFSv4 server by deferring the freeing of compound operation buffers until after an RCU grace period. The rpc_status netlink dumpit walks in-flight requests and reads opnums from these buffers, but the buffers are freed synchronously before the dumpit has finished reading them. By using kvfree_rcu_mightsleep() instead of vfree(), the patch ensures that the buffers persist in memory until after an RCU grace period, preventing a use-after-free bug.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/8] nfsd: hold rcu across localio cmpxchg retry
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS server (nfsd) where a freed nfsd_file object's memory is reused, causing a reference count bump to appear successful and leading to dereferencing of freed memory. The fix involves holding RCU read lock across a cmpxchg retry block to prevent the slab page backing the freed nfsd_file from being recycled during the retry. This ensures that the refcount increment operation operates on valid memory.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/8] nfs/localio: fix ref leak on nfs_uuid_add_file failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a reference leak in the NFS/localio code when nfs_uuid_add_file() fails. The issue occurs when nfs_uuid_put() tears down uuid->net while nfs_uuid_add_file() is still racing, causing the function to return -ENXIO without publishing nfl->nfs_uuid via rcu_assign_pointer(). As a result, the caller-owned file reference and its net reference are leaked, blocking nfsd_net and netns teardown. The patch fixes this by releasing the caller-owned file ref and its net ref through the existing helper, using a stack-local RCU pointer to xchg it out, and returning -ENXIO so callers do not dereference a localio whose slot has been cleared.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/8] nfsd: guard nfsd_serv deref in nfsd_file_net_dispose
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd service thread loop where it attempts to wake up another thread by calling svc_wake_up(nn->nfsd_serv) even when nn->nfsd_serv is NULL. The fix involves loading nn->nfsd_serv into a local pointer and only calling svc_wake_up() if the pointer is non-NULL, matching the pattern in nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed(). This patch addresses an issue introduced by a previous commit that left work of closing files to a work queue.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/8] nfsd: widen nfsd_genl_rqstp address fields to sockaddr_storage
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the nfsd kernel module where IPv6 addresses were being truncated when sent to userspace via netlink attributes. The issue was caused by casting a 16-byte sockaddr field to a 28-byte sockaddr_in6 pointer, which extended beyond the end of the field into an adjacent member. To fix this, the patch widens the address fields in the nfsd_genl_rqstp struct from sockaddr to sockaddr_storage, ensuring that IPv6 addresses are captured fully and preventing uninitialized bytes from being leaked through netlink.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/8] nfsd: fix refcount leak in nfsd_file_lru_add on insertion failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a latent bug in the NFS server that causes a reference count leak when inserting an nfsd_file into the LRU fails. The leak occurs because the reference count is incremented but never decremented, causing the file cache entry to be retained indefinitely. The patch adds a compensating refcount decrement on failure and changes the WARN_ON condition to prevent log flooding. Additionally, it returns early on failure to avoid unnecessary calls to nfsd_file_schedule_laundrette().

series_items.subject: [PATCH 7/8] nfsd: fix fcache_disposal UAF by inlining dispose state into nfsd_net
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free (UAF) bug in the NFS daemon's file cache disposal mechanism by inlining the dispose state into the nfsd_net struct, eliminating the need for a separately allocated struct. This change prevents the global shrinker, laundrette, and fsnotify callbacks from dereferencing a freed pointer. The patch also removes the alloc/free helpers and simplifies the per-net file cache management.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 8/8] nfsd: hold net namespace reference in nfsd_file
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a use-after-free bug in the NFS daemon by holding a reference to the net namespace for each file. The fix involves taking a net-namespace reference when allocating an nfsd_file and releasing it when freeing the file. This ensures that the net namespace is always valid, even if the file is isolated from the hash table and LRU. The patch also updates the nfsd_file_put_local function to release the net namespace reference before dropping the file reference.

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Al Viro noted that the patch introduces a potential scalability issue due to shared cachelines between nfsd_file_alloc() and nfsd_file_free(), which may have implications for kernel performance on systems with net namespace enabled.
review_comments.raw_body:
That means that each nfsd_file_alloc()/nfsd_file_free() is now touching
the same cacheline on kernels with netns enabled.  Scalability implications
might be interesting...

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a concern about releasing the net namespace reference in nfsd_file_free() and agreed to explore alternative solutions.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's a very good point.

There are some alternatives. Let me play around with them and see if I
can find something better.

Thanks for the review,
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

review_comments.author: Jeff Layton (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged a valid concern about excessive get/put_net activity, agreed to implement a solution in v2 by only taking net references for GC'ed files.
review_comments.raw_body:
That's definitely a valid concern. I looked at a couple of alternatives
but they turned out to be pretty nasty.

One thing we can do is only take a net reference for GC'ed files, since
we don't need a reference for the others. That would cut down some of
the get/put_net activity, but it's still likely to be substantial. I'll
plan to implement that for v2.

I'll keep thinking about other ways to do this, but I think we're stuck
taking net references for at least some of these for now.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses several bugs in the NFS daemon, including a refcount leak and a UAF bug in filecache disposal. The patches simplify the filecache disposal mechanism to prevent these issues. Additionally, one patch fixes a scalability concern by holding net namespace references for nfsd_file objects.

review_comments.author: Al Viro
review_comments.summary: Raised a concern about the scalability implications of holding net namespace references for nfsd_file objects. Suggested that this could lead to cacheline contention on kernels with netns enabled.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Mon, Jun 01, 2026 at 01:31:11PM -0400, Jeff Layton wrote:
> Take a net-namespace reference in nfsd_file_alloc() (get_net) and
> release it in nfsd_file_free() (put_net), so that nf_net is always
> valid for files that the GC or shrinker has isolated from the hash
> table and LRU -- which __nfsd_file_cache_purge() cannot see.
> 
> Without this, nf_net can dangle for in-flight files whose net namespace
> is torn down concurrently, causing a use-after-free when
> nfsd_file_dispose_list_delayed() calls net_generic(nf->nf_net, ...).
> 
> Because nfsd_file_free() now calls put_net(nf->nf_net), the old
> nfsd_file_put_local() pattern of returning nf->nf_net after
> nfsd_file_put() is unsafe -- put_net() could theoretically drop the
> last net namespace reference, leaving the returned pointer stale.
> Fix this by moving the nfsd_net_put() call into nfsd_file_put_local()
> itself, before the nfsd_file_put() that may trigger nfsd_file_free().
> The function now returns void and the caller no longer needs to handle
> the net reference.

That means that each nfsd_file_alloc()/nfsd_file_free() is now touching
the same cacheline on kernels with netns enabled.  Scalability implications
might be interesting...

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/6] nfsd: medium-severity bugfixes

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/6] nfsd: size fh_verify server sockaddr slot by xpt_locallen
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the NFS daemon's tracepoint handling by sizing the server sockaddr slot correctly when copying from xpt_local. The issue was that the slot was sized by xpt_remotelen, but filled from xpt_local using xpt_locallen, leading to potential buffer overflows or underflows. The fix is to size the server slot with xpt_locallen, matching the copy length. This patch only affects the nfsd_fh_verify and nfsd_fh_verify_err tracepoints, which are now consistent with other NFSD tracepoints that record the server address.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/6] nfsd: release path refs on follow_down() error
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFS server by releasing path references when follow_down() fails. The issue occurs because the error handling arm in nfsd_cross_mnt() does not call path_put(&path) to release the references taken on entry, leading to inflated mount and dentry counts that can block umount and pin dentries against the shrinker. The patch adds a call to path_put(&path) before the goto out statement in the err < 0 arm to fix this issue.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/6] nfsd: fix nfsd_file leak on inter-server COPY setup failure
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFS server (nfsd) caused by failed inter-server COPY operations. When nfsd4_setup_inter_ssc() fails, it directly returns an error code without releasing the reference to the nf_dst object, leading to a file leak. The fix involves setting the status and jumping to the out label instead of returning directly, ensuring that the reference is properly released.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/6] nfsd: fix dentry ref leak on V4ROOT export filehandle lookup
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a memory leak in the NFSv3 filehandle handling code. When a V4ROOT export's fsid is targeted, the dentry reference is not properly released, leading to a memory leak on every request. The patch adds a call to dput(dentry) when NFSEXP_V4ROOT is detected, ensuring that the reference count is correctly updated.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/6] nfsd: release OPEN-decoded posix ACLs via op_release
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a double-free bug in the NFSv4 server, where posix_acl objects are not properly released when an OPEN compound is short-circuited due to a non-seqid-mutating error. The fix involves adding an op_release function for OP_OPEN and updating the nfsd4_open_omfg function to call it.

review_comments.summary: Identified a gap in the original fix that leaks ACLs on the v4.0 replay path. Suggested releasing op->u on this branch as well.

review_comments.summary: Acknowledged the issue and agreed to fix it.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Sun, 2026-05-31 at 11:54 -0400, Chuck Lever wrote:
> On Sun, May 31, 2026, at 8:07 AM, Jeff Layton wrote:
> > From: Chris Mason <clm@meta.com>
> 
> > Remove the matching posix_acl_release() pair from nfsd4_open()'s
> > out_err: label: the compound dispatcher calls op_release
> > unconditionally after every op, [...]
> 
> The double-free fix is right, but op_release is not called
> unconditionally after every op, and that gap leaks the ACLs on the
> v4.0 replay path.
> 
> op_release runs only at the release: label inside
> nfsd4_encode_operation().  The compound loop skips that encoder on
> a replay:
> 
>         if (op->status == nfserr_replay_me) {
>                 nfsd4_encode_replay(resp->xdr, op);     /* no op_release */
>                 ...
>         } else {
>                 nfsd4_encode_operation(resp, op);       /* op_release here */
>         }
> 
> So every ACL-bearing v4.0 OPEN retransmit leaks two posix_acl refs.
> 
> Please release op->u on the replay branch too:
> 
>         if (op->status == nfserr_replay_me) {
>                 op->replay = &cstate->replay_owner->so_replay;
>                 nfsd4_encode_replay(resp->xdr, op);
>                 status = op->status = op->replay->rp_status;
>                 if (op->opdesc->op_release)

>         }
>

> 
> I've applied the other 5 in this series, so you can just resend
> this one.
> 

Good catch, will fix.

I initially had hoped that we could rely on the fact that a v4.0 client
couldn't issue an OPEN with a POSIX ACL attribute in it, but it turns
out that knfsd doesn't gate attributes by minorversion.

Maybe it should? That's a separate changeset though if so.
-- 
Jeff Layton <jlayton@kernel.org>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/6] nfsd: fix layout fence worker double-reference race
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a double-reference race condition in the NFSd layout fence worker. The issue arises when the workqueue core clears WORK_STRUCT_PENDING before invoking the callback, allowing lm_breaker_timedout() to take a duplicate reference on the layout stateid. To fix this, the patch introduces an ls_fence_inflight boolean flag that is set atomically with refcount_inc_not_zero() under ls_lock and cleared under ls_lock before nfs4_put_stid() on every exit path. This ensures that a fence worker invocation already in progress does not coalesce with a new one, preventing a memory leak of the layout stateid.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series addresses medium-severity bugs in the NFS server code, including memory leaks and refcounting issues that can be triggered remotely. The patches fix problems such as file handle verification, path reference management, and layout fence worker synchronization. The changes aim to prevent potential buffer overruns and improve overall stability of the NFS server.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools and zero-copy

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/10] fuse: separate next request fetching from sending logic
series_items.patch_summary: This patch separates the logic of fetching the next request from sending it, making the control flow clearer and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. It removes duplicated code in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and introduces a new function fuse_uring_get_next_fuse_req() to fetch the next request. The send operation is now handled by a separate function fuse_uring_send(). This change simplifies the logic for handling requests and improves maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying to ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers to a ring in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) io-uring implementation. It extracts the header copying into a new function called copy_header_to_ring(), which centralizes error handling and rate-limited logging, making the code more clear and maintainable. The patch modifies several functions that interact with the ring, replacing direct calls to copy_to_user() with calls to the new function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/10] fuse: refactor io-uring header copying from ring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the logic for copying headers from a ring in the fuse kernel module. It extracts this logic into a new function called copy_header_from_ring(), which makes it clearer and more centralized, and also handles errors and rate-limited logging. This change is part of a larger series of patches to improve the io-uring functionality in the fuse module.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/10] fuse: use enum types for header copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the interface for copying headers in the FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) implementation by introducing enum types to identify which part of the header needs to be copied. This change will simplify both kernel-space and user-space header addresses copying when buffer rings are added, making it easier to manage and maintain the code.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/10] fuse: refactor setting up copy state for payload copying
series_items.patch_summary: This patch refactors the code for setting up copy state in the FUSE implementation of dev_uring by introducing a new helper function `setup_fuse_copy_state`. This function encapsulates the logic for importing user buffers and initializing the copy state. The existing code is simplified and made more modular, reducing duplication and improving maintainability.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/10] fuse: use named constants for io-uring iovec indices
series_items.patch_summary: This patch replaces magic indices 0 and 1 in the iovec array with named constants FUSE_URING_IOV_HEADERS and FUSE_URING_IOV_PAYLOAD, making the code self-documenting and preparing for future buffer ring support. The patch modifies several functions in the fuse module to use these new constants instead of hardcoded indices. This change improves code readability and maintainability by eliminating magic numbers and making it easier to understand the purpose of each index.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/10] fuse: add io-uring buffer pools (implemented as buffer ring)
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for buffer pools in the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing the server to pass a contiguous allocation of buffers that can be used on demand by the kernel. This decouples the number of entries from the number of payload buffers required, reducing memory usage and enabling future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption. The server must set the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag during registration and provide the payload buffer pool through an iovec payload in the first sqe. The kernel manages buffer selection and recycling using a buffer ring data structure.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for using registered buffers as buffer pools in io-uring, allowing servers to use the same pinned pages for subsequent I/O operations and reducing overhead. Registered buffers require enabling the FUSE_URING_BUFPOOL flag and setting the FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS init flag during registration. The patch also includes benchmark results showing a 10% throughput improvement for direct randreads and other improvements for different types of reads.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/10] fuse: add zero-copy over io-uring
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds zero-copy support to the fuse io-uring implementation in the Linux kernel. It eliminates memory copies between userspace, the kernel, and the fuse server for read/write operations by allowing the server to directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer. The patch requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS capabilities and is gated behind these requirements. The zero-copy write path involves registering relevant pages in the server's io-uring registered buffer table, operating on these pages using fixed buffer operations, and unregistering them when the request completes.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/10] docs: fuse: add io-uring buffer pool and zero-copy documentation
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds documentation to the Linux kernel's FUSE (Filesystem in Userspace) module for using buffer pools and zero-copy functionality with io-uring. The documentation explains how to use buffer pools, which decouple the relationship between entries and payload buffers, allowing for more efficient memory usage. It also describes how the server manages the buffer pool through a buffer ring, enabling features like incremental buffer consumption and multiple buffer sizes per ring. This patch aims to improve performance by reducing unnecessary copies of data between kernel and user space.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series adds buffer pools and zero-copy functionality to the fuse io-uring implementation, allowing servers to pass in a managed buffer pool that reduces memory usage requirements and enables future optimizations. It also allows for direct access to client pages or page cache folios without copying data through an intermediary buffer, requiring CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges. The changes are based on registered buffers and zero-copy, which can provide significant throughput improvements depending on the server's per-request latency and backing I/O characteristics.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing a concern about duplicated logic in fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring() and fuse_uring_send(). They simplified the logic by removing duplicate code from fuse_uring_send_next_to_ring(), decoupling request fetching from the send operation, and reducing unnecessary parameter passing. The patch removes 50 lines of code and adds 28 new lines.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the tight coupling between ents and buffers in fuse io-uring by explaining their new buffer pool design, which decouples them and allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and translating virtual addresses per i/o request, explaining that registered buffers eliminate this overhead by pinning pages upfront. The author provided details on how to use registered buffers, including setting flags in the registration sqe and following standard protocol for using registered buffers.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the zero-copy implementation's complexity and overhead, explaining that it requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and FUSE_URING_REGISTERED_BUFFERS to prevent partially-optimized modes. The author provided detailed request flow diagrams for both write and read paths, highlighting how pages are registered and unregistered in the io_uring registered buffer table.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed Bernd's concern about registering ring entries without payloads, explaining that entries may be used for servicing requests even if there are less buffers than the number of entries, and that it would allow multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer concurrently. The author also mentioned that supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring and some requests not needing payload buffers justify decoupling entries from buffers.

mapping between ents and buffers. There's also some requests that
don't need to use payload buffers at all (eg flush, fsync, rmdir,
etc).  As well for registered buffers / buffer pinning, it is more
optimal for the buffer pool to be allocated contiguously and pinned
contiguously rather than passed in one by one as a 1:1 entry:buffer

I don't think this is a good idea. The server would have to
guesstimate this as it doesn't know the request size until it sees the
request. The server could speculatively pre-register ring ents ahead
of time but there's no guarantee that would be the right size for the
ring entry either. And once added, if the workload pattern changes,
then the ent and allocated buffer may not be useful anymore. This also
adds (imo needless) complexity to the kernel side. Additionally, the
dynamic ring ents would in the ideal case have payload buffers pinned,
which there is not an non-messy way to do (for buffer pools, the pool
buffer pinning goes through io-uring registered buffers infrastructure
prior to being passed to fuse at registration time). Imo the better
way if you wanted to dynamically add ents would be to add a new buffer
pool to the ring.

Please take a look at patch 8 ([PATCH v3 08/10] fuse: support

is contiguously allocated and registered as an io-uring registered
buffer, and then passed to fuse and by that point, the buffers have
already been pinned by io-uring.

Even irrespective of page pinning, it would still be good to pass the
buffers contiguously up front as a pool instead of per-ent, as the
purpose of this series is to decouple the 1:1 entry:buffer mapping. Eg
if the request an ent is servicing does not require buffers, a buffer

> - Fixed size, allocated at startup, cannot be easily increased or decreased

>
> Entries with payload

>

I think perhaps there is a misunderstanding of the patches in this series.

This series implements buffer pools, which decouple the 1:1 mapping
between ents and buffers that currently exists. Right now, ents and
buffers are tightly coupled where each entry has its own dedicated
payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where each buffer
must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size.

This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool where the buffers
are not statically mapped to an ent at registration time. This
decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use
fuse-io-uring. It is the necessary stepping stone for future
optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption (allowing
multiple entries to use non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) and supporting multi-size buffers per ring.

Thanks,
Joanne

It's not just about the memory savings. Fundamentally, I believe it's
the wrong abstraction to statically couple ents to buffers 1:1 as a
fixed mapping. Entries and buffers serve different purposes. Entries
handle request flow, buffers hold data. Statically coupling them 1:1
is inflexible, introduces unnecessary complexity, and imo has no added
benefit. This series decouples these two concepts. It allows
dynamically adding ents in the future as *generic* ents not tied to a
particular buffer size (eg the added ent can be used to service *any*
request size, not just the size of the buffer it's tied to). With
tying ents to buffers, the server is stuck with a static distribution
that can't adapt to workload changes.

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary: The author is addressing feedback on the buffer allocation mechanism in fuse io-uring, specifically the issue that most request types require less than the maximum payload size, leading to inefficient memory usage. The author proposes decoupling entries from payload buffers and using a pool of dynamically selected buffers instead. They are seeking input on this approach.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59 PM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>
> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>
> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>
> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>
> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
> registered buffers.
>
> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
> in [1].
>
> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
> from eliminating the memcpy.
>
> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>
> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
> dominated.
>
> The benchmarks were run using:
>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>
> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
> submitted upstream.
>
> Thanks,
> Joanne
>
> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>

Miklos,

This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
appreciate your input on the direction.

Background:
In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.

Problem that needs to be solved:
Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.

Approach A:
Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
(multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
concurrently) as optimizations in the future.

Approach B:
Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
added as a separate pool.

I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

Miklos, what are your thoughts?

Thanks,
Joanne

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary: Bernd Schubert noted that the current implementation ties each entry in fuse io-uring to a full 1 MiB payload buffer, regardless of actual need, resulting in excessive memory usage on systems with many entries. He proposed decoupling entries from payload buffers as an approach to solve this problem.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 5/29/26 18:32, Joanne Koong wrote:
> On Fri, May 22, 2026 at 1:59\u202fPM Joanne Koong <joannelkoong@gmail.com> wrote:
>>
>> This series adds buffer pools (implemented internally as a buffer ring) and
>> zero-copy to fuse io-uring.
>>
>> Prior to this series, ents and buffers are tightly coupled where each entry
>> has its own dedicated payload buffer, requiring N buffers for N entries where
>> each buffer must be large enough to accomodate the maximum payload size. This
>> is suboptimal as most request types require vastly less bytes than the maximum
>> payload size and some requests do not require payload buffers at all.
>>
>> This series allows servers to pass in a buffer pool (internally managed as a
>> buffer ring) that the kernel will use for servicing ents/requests. This
>> decoupling reduces the memory usage requirements needed to use fuse-io-uring.
>> It allows for future optimizations such as incremental buffer consumption and
>> supporting multiple buffer sizes per ring.
>>
>> This series also allows servers to use io-uring registered buffers as the
>> buffer pool which eliminates the overhead of pinning/unpinning user pages and
>> translating virtual addreses per i/o request.
>>
>> This series additionally adds zero copy to fuse io-uring. The server can
>> directly access client pages or page cache folios without copying data through
>> an intermediary buffer. This requires CAP_SYS_ADMIN privileges and using
>> registered buffers.
>>
>> This series has a dependency on io-uring registered bvec buffers changes
>> in [1].
>>
>> The throughput improvements from registered buffers and zero-copy depends on
>> how much of the server's per-request latency is spent on data copying vs
>> backing I/O. When backing I/O dominates, the saved memcpy is a negligible
>> fraction of overall latency. Please also note that for the server to
>> read/write into the zero-copied pages, the read/write must go through io-uring
>> as an IORING_OP_READ_FIXED / IORING_OP_WRITE_FIXED operation. If the
>> server's backing I/O is instantaneous (eg served from cache), the
>> overhead of the additional io_uring operation may negate the savings
>> from eliminating the memcpy.
>>
>> In benchmarks using passthrough_hp on a high-performance NVMe-backed
>> system, using registered buffers showed around a 10% throughput improvement
>> for direct randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2400 MiB/s), a 4% improvement for direct
>> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2620 MiB/s), a 8% improvement for buffered
>> randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2280 MiB/s), and a 6% improvement for buffered
>> sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2670 MiB/s).
>>
>> Zero-copy showed around a 35% throughput improvement for direct
>> randreads (~2150 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for direct
>> sequential reads (~2510 MiB/s to ~2900 MiB/s), a 15% improvement for
>> buffered randreads (~2100 MiB/s to ~2470 MiB/s), and a 10% improvement
>> for buffered sequential reads (~2500 MiB/s to ~2750 MiB/s). I didn't see
>> enough of a clear improvement for writes due to write latency being I/O
>> dominated.
>>
>> The benchmarks were run using:
>>   fio --name=test_run --ioengine=sync --rw=rand{read,write} --bs=1M
>>   --size=1G --numjobs=2 --ramp_time=30 --group_reporting=1
>>
>> The libfuse changes can be found in [2]. To test the server, run:
>> sudo ~/libfuse/build/example/passthrough_hp ~/src ~/mounts/tmp
>> --nopassthrough -o io_uring_zero_copy -o io_uring_q_depth=8
>> Once this series is merged, the libfuse changes will be tidied up and
>> submitted upstream.
>>
>> Thanks,
>> Joanne
>>
>> [1] https://lore.kernel.org/io-uring/20260402160929.2749744-1-joannelkoong@gmail.com/T/#t
>> [2] https://github.com/joannekoong/libfuse/commits/zero_copy_v3/
>>
> 
> Miklos,
> 
> This series has stalled on an architectural question and I'd
> appreciate your input on the direction.
> 
> Background:
> In fuse io-uring, entries are reusable communication slots between the
> kernel and the daemon. When a request needs to be sent from the kernel
> to the daemon, it is assigned to an available entry. The entry carries
> the request to userspace, and the response comes back through the same
> entry. The number of entries determines how many requests can be
> serviced concurrently. Currently, when the daemon registers an entry
> during fuse io-uring setup, it passes in a payload buffer (sized to
> the maximum payload, typically 1 MiB) alongside it. The buffer is tied
> to the entry for the lifetime of the connection.
> 
> Problem that needs to be solved:
> Most request types (lookup, getattr, open, etc) require far less than
> the maximum payload size, and some (flush, fsync, etc) require no
> payload buffer at all. Every entry reserves a full 1 MiB buffer
> regardless of actual need. On systems with many entries, this memory
> cost scales linearly - for example, 64 entries across 8 queues
> requires 512 MiB of payload buffers alone.
> 
> Approach A:
> Decouple entries from payload buffers. Entries become generic - any
> entry can use any buffer from the pool. At setup time, the daemon
> passes in a contiguous pool of memory, and the kernel dynamically
> selects and recycles buffers from this pool as entries are assigned
> requests. Entries servicing requests with no payloads consume no
> buffer, and the pool can have fewer buffers than entries. This
> supports adding multi-size buffers and incremental buffer consumption
> (multiple entries using non-overlapping regions of the same buffer
> concurrently) as optimizations in the future.
> 
> Approach B:
> Keep the 1:1 entry-buffer coupling, but allow entries to be registered
> with different payload buffer sizes at setup time. The daemon would
> register entries with smaller buffers for metadata-heavy workloads and
> larger buffers for data-heavy ones. This keeps the existing model of
> passing in a payload buffer during entry registration at setup time
> and requires no uapi additions. Each entry registration adds buffer
> capacity whereas with buffer pools, buffer capacity would have to be
> added as a separate pool.
> 
> I want to make sure I'm representing both approaches fairly - Bernd,
> please correct anything I've gotten wrong.

I think for B "at setup time" is not entirely correct - buffers in the
current model buffers and entries can be registered at run time.


I have been thinking about approach C, which I think is in between A and B.
I my mind approach C does the registration per entry, but payload
buffers get added to a buf array, if a payload is set.
Max array size would be negotiated as in your approach or maybe better
with a config SQE. Different IO size could achieved by multiple buf arrays.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Bernd Schubert
review_comments.summary:
Bernd Schubert agrees with decoupling entries from buffers, but expresses disagreement on how buffers are passed in, preferring a contiguous pool upfront over individual buffer passing.

Bernd Schubert expressed concerns that the contiguous buffer pool approach would make it difficult to implement dynamic registration and unregistering of buffers in libfuse, as it wouldn't be clear how to register on demand and unregistering would be even harder. He also noted that if races during registration are present, the current scheme's dynamic registration from the kernel perspective wouldn't change anything.

reviewer reminded that Miklos requested dynamic registration of buffers, which was a change from the initial static approach taken from ublk tag model
review_comments.raw_body:
Well let's say, I don't see what speaks against decoupling it. The
memory saving effect is due to the special form of different request
sizes (0 and max payload).

---

My issue with this approach is still the same, incrementally registering
memory when one needs it is hard. Example, I have a few external SSDs
that are connected most of the time, but I only need them every few
days. Why would I need to spend memory for the fuse transport all the
time? The ideal situation would be: libfuse detects heavy usage and
registers more entries and buffers.
Current fuse kernel supports it. I just didn't get to it to implement
dynamic registration in libfuse yet - a matter of time.
Missing is some form of kernel unregister, although easy to add, if
unregister is treated as request OP.

With a contiguous buffer pool I wouldn't know how to register on demand
and unregistering is even harder.

Also, if we still have races during register phase, doing it on demand
wouldn't change anything in the current scheme - either the race is
there or it is not, because current scheme is dynamic registration from
kernel point of view.


No we need to keep that old scheme one way or the other, but we could
deprecate one form and avoid adding new features (like different buffer
sizes). However, if buffer pools have a limited usability, once needs to
do those features for both approaches.

---

Just a short reminder that it was actually Miklos to request the dynamic
registration way. Initially it was all static, taken from the ublk tag
model. Making it dynamic added some headache, but in the end I also
noticed that it can be useful.


Thanks,
Bernd

review_comments.author: Joanne Koong (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author responded to Bernd's feedback about passing buffers in a decoupled manner by explaining that registering/unregistering buffer pools or queues can handle the scenario, and believes it won't be more complicated with contiguous buffer pools.

Author acknowledges that the disagreement about how buffers get passed in still exists, requests clarification from Miklos on whether his previous stance is relevant given the decoupling of entries and buffers.
review_comments.raw_body:
For the scenario you mentioned above, you could register/unregister a
buffer pool within one queue or alternatively, register/unregister a
new queue, depending on the workload. I think the details can be
worked out once we agree on the core design, but I don't think it
would be more complicated with contiguous buffer pools.

---

I wasn't there when that conversation happened a few years ago, so I'm
not sure if Miklos's stance still pertains here where ents are
decoupled from buffers. I think we need Miklos to clarify this for us.

Thanks,
Joanne

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 1/9] mm: list_lru: fix set_shrinker_bit() call during race with cgroup deletion
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series addresses a long-standing bug in the list_lru code, where the shrinker bit is set on the wrong memory cgroup during a race condition between list_lru_add() and cgroup deletion. The fix involves returning the effective memcg from the locking function and having the caller use that. This change affects the mm/list_lru.c file.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 2/9] mm: list_lru: lock_list_lru_of_memcg() cannot return NULL if !skip_empty
series_items.patch_summary: This patch removes unnecessary NULL checks in the lock_list_lru_of_memcg() function, which is used to acquire a lock for list additions and deletions. The function will not fail to acquire the lock, so these checks are no longer needed. This change simplifies the code and makes it more efficient.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 3/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate unlock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch deduplicates the unlock_list_lru() function by consolidating its implementation into a single inline function, eliminating duplicate code in both MEMCG and !MEMCG variants. The function is responsible for unlocking the lock associated with a list_lru_one structure, taking into account whether interrupts are disabled or not. This change simplifies the codebase and reduces maintenance overhead.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 4/9] mm: list_lru: move list dead check to lock_list_lru_of_memcg()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for a race condition between list LRU operations and cgroup deletion to the caller of lock_list_lru_of_memcg(). This is done by removing the dead list check from the function itself, allowing for future unification of the lock_list_lru() variants. The change affects only the MEMCG variant of lock_list_lru(), which needs to handle this specific race condition.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 5/9] mm: list_lru: deduplicate lock_list_lru()
series_items.patch_summary: The patch deduplicates the lock_list_lru() function by sharing a common implementation between MEMCG and non-MEMCG paths, reducing code duplication.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 6/9] mm: list_lru: introduce caller locking for additions and deletions
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces caller locking for additions and deletions in the list_lru API. It provides normal and irqsafe locking primitives as well as caller-locked variants of the addition and deletion functions to allow callers to keep auxiliary state synchronized with the LRU state. This is necessary because some users, such as the THP shrinker, require this synchronization to maintain consistency. The patch adds new locking functions and modifies existing ones to support this feature.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 7/9] mm: list_lru: introduce folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc()
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new helper function called folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() that allocates list_lru heads for shrinkable folios under the memory cgroup (memcg) they are associated with. The function is designed to handle the complexities of accessing the memcg of a folio, which requires either RCU protection or pinning the cgroup. It aims to make the existence check lightweight under RCU and only pin the memcg when necessary for allocation. This patch is part of a larger series that prepares the THP shrinker to use list_lru, and it has been reviewed by several kernel developers.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 8/9] mm: memory: flatten alloc_anon_folio() retry loop
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series aims to improve the code organization and readability in the memory management subsystem by flattening nested if statements, removing unnecessary complexity, and simplifying the logic of the alloc_anon_folio() function.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 9/9] mm: switch deferred split shrinker to list_lru
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates the deferred split shrinker to use list_lru, which properly sets up per-node and per-cgroup lists. This change addresses a suboptimal handling of cgroups in the deferred split queue, where large pages on other nodes were being split unnecessarily. The new approach streamlines list operations and reclaim walks, and is used by other major shrinkers already. The patch also adds calls to create node heads for the cgroup at once when splittable pages are created, eliminating the need for special care in migration code between nodes.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series, version 5, aims to switch the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) shrinker to use list_lru instead of the current implementation. The changes include fixing a long-standing issue in set_shrinker_bit() where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg instead of the ancestor that received the item. Other changes include adapting to the new lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature, propagating folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure, and fixing a use-after-free bug in deferred_split_isolate(). The patches have been rebased onto the latest mm-unstable tree.

review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that the deferred split queue handles cgroups suboptimally by being per-NUMA node or per-cgroup instead of their intersection, which can lead to large pages being split on other nodes; requested conversion to list_lru, which sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists and streamlines reclaim walks.

On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> When list_lru_add() races with cgroup deletion, the shrinker bit is set
> on the wrong group and lost. This can cause a shrinker run to miss the
> cgroup that actually has the object.
> 
> When the passed in memcg is dead, the function finds the first non-dead
> parent from the passed in memcg and adds the object there; but the
> shrinker bit is set on the memcg that was passed in.
> 
> This bug is as old as the shrinker bitmap itself.
> 
> Fix it by returning the "effective" memcg from the locking function, and
> have the caller use that.
> 
> Fixes: fae91d6d8be5 ("mm/list_lru.c: set bit in memcg shrinker bitmap on first list_lru item appearance")
> Reported-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>

>

On 27/05/2026 21:45, Johannes Weiner wrote:
> The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
> queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
> on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
> can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
> 
>         alloc/unmap
>           deferred_split_folio()
>             list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>             set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
> 
>         for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>           mem_cgroup_iter()
>             shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>               shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                 if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                   deferred_split_scan()
>                     walks memcg->split_queue
> 
> The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
> has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
> by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
> 
> list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
> streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
> widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
> queue as well.
> 
> The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
> object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
> folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
> created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
> 
> These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
> the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.

>The deferred split queue handles cgroups in a suboptimal fashion. The
>queue is per-NUMA node or per-cgroup, not the intersection. That means
>on a cgrouped system, a node-restricted allocation entering reclaim
>can end up splitting large pages on other nodes:
>
>        alloc/unmap
>          deferred_split_folio()
>            list_add_tail(memcg->split_queue)
>            set_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>
>        for_each_zone_zonelist_nodemask(restricted_nodes)
>          mem_cgroup_iter()
>            shrink_slab(node, memcg)
>              shrink_slab_memcg(node, memcg)
>                if test_shrinker_bit(memcg, node, deferred_shrinker_id)
>                  deferred_split_scan()
>                    walks memcg->split_queue
>
>The shrinker bit adds an imperfect guard rail. As soon as the cgroup
>has a single large page on the node of interest, all large pages owned
>by that memcg, including those on other nodes, will be split.
>
>list_lru properly sets up per-node, per-cgroup lists. As a bonus, it
>streamlines a lot of the list operations and reclaim walks. It's used
>widely by other major shrinkers already. Convert the deferred split
>queue as well.
>
>The list_lru per-memcg heads are instantiated on demand when the first
>object of interest is allocated for a cgroup, by calling
>folio_memcg_alloc_deferred(). Add calls to where splittable pages are
>created: anon faults, swapin faults, khugepaged collapse.
>
>These calls create all possible node heads for the cgroup at once, so
>the migration code (between nodes) doesn't need any special care.

review_comments.raw_body:
Hi Johannes,

On Wed, May 27, 2026 at 04:45:07PM -0400, Johannes Weiner wrote:
>This is version 5 of switching the THP shrinker to list_lru.
>
>Core of the new version is the list_lru/set_shrinker_bit fix up front,
>which minimally affects later patches; and a rebase onto the latest
>mm-unstable - replaced alloc_swap_folio() with __swap_cache_alloc().
>
>The changes seemed small enough that *I chose to keep the review tags
>from v4*. Please shout if you object to this!
>
>Changes in v5:
>- patch 1 is a new fix for a very old, pre-existing set_shrinker_bit()
>  problem in list_lru, where the bit can be set on a dying child memcg
>  instead of the ancestor that actually received the item. Pointed out
>  by Usama Arif and Sashiko; fix it first to make it minimally
>  backportable and so the conversion is safe.
>- patches 6 and 9 adapt to that fix's new memcg-by-reference
>  lock_list_lru_of_memcg() signature
>- collapse_huge_page(): propagate folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() failure
>  as SCAN_ALLOC_HUGE_PAGE_FAIL instead of leaking SCAN_SUCCEED and
>  falsely reporting a successful MADV_COLLAPSE (Usama Arif, Sashiko)
>- deferred_split_isolate(): fix a UAF by reading folio state before
>  list_lru_isolate(); once removed, a racing folio_put() frees the
>  folio via the lockless list_empty() check while we still touch its
>  flags and stats (Sashiko)
>- rebased to mm-unstable of 2026-05-27, which simplifies the flatten
>  prep patch (now anon-only, as alloc_swap_folio() was folded into the
>  new __swap_cache_alloc()) and moves the swap-side
>  folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() hook into __swap_cache_alloc(). Kairui,
>  I would appreciate an eyeball on that.
>
>Changes in v4:
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with mem_cgroup_disabled() to fix
>  NULL deref in __memcg_list_lru_alloc() when booting with
>  cgroup_disable=memory (e.g., kdump capture kernel) -- reported and
>  tested by Mikhail Zaslonko on s390 and x86
>- flatten if (folio) branches in alloc_swap_folio() and alloc_anon_folio()
>  in a prep patch so the list_lru allocation additions are a clean minimal
>  diff (Lorenzo)
>- folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() moved out of alloc_charge_folio() into the
>  anon-only collapse_huge_page() path; collapse_file() shares that helper
>  but its pages don't go on the THP shrinker queue (David)
>- guard folio_memcg_alloc_deferred() with order > 1; mTHPs below order-2
>  can't be queued on the deferred split list (David)
>- make deferred_split_lru static, hide behind folio_memcg_alloc_deferred()
>  wrapper with GFP_KERNEL (Lorenzo)
>- rename l -> lru throughout huge_memory.c (Lorenzo)
>- kdoc for folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (Lorenzo)
>- list_lru_lock_irq()/unlock_irq()/add_irq() irq-disabling variants;
>  use list_lru_add_irq() in deferred_split_scan() (Lorenzo)
>- reorder shrinker_free() before list_lru_destroy() (Lorenzo)
>
>Changes in v3:
>- dedicated lockdep_key for irqsafe deferred_split_lru.lock (syzbot)
>- conditional list_lru ops in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (syzbot)
>- annotate runs of inscrutable false, NULL, false function arguments (David)
>- rename to folio_memcg_list_lru_alloc() (David)
>
>Changes in v2:
>- explicit rcu_read_lock() in __folio_freeze_and_split_unmapped() (Usama)
>- split out list_lru prep bits (Dave)
>
>The open-coded deferred split queue has issues. It's not NUMA-aware
>(when cgroup is enabled), and it's more complicated in the callsites
>interacting with it. Switching to list_lru fixes the NUMA problem and
>streamlines things. It also simplifies planned shrinker work.
>
>Patch 1 fixes a pre-existing list_lru bug where the shrinker bit is
>set on the caller's memcg rather than the ancestor whose sublist the
>item actually lands on after a walk-up. Standalone, backportable; the
>rest of the series depends on it.
>
>Patches 2-5 are cleanups and small refactors in list_lru code. They're
>basically independent, but make the THP shrinker conversion easier.
>
>Patch 6 extends the list_lru API to allow the caller to control the
>locking scope. The THP shrinker has private state it needs to keep
>synchronized with the LRU state.
>
>Patch 7 extends the list_lru API with a convenience helper to do
>list_lru head allocation (memcg_list_lru_alloc) when coming from a
>folio. Anon THPs are instantiated in several places, and with the
>folio reparenting patches pending, folio_memcg() access is now a more
>delicate dance. This avoids having to replicate that dance everywhere.
>
>Patch 8 flattens the alloc_anon_folio() retry loop so the next patch's
>list_lru hook lands as a clean addition rather than nested deep inside
>an if (folio) block.
>
>Patch 9 finally switches the deferred_split_queue to list_lru.

As the changelog above says, the old queue is per-memcg only, rather
than per-memcg-per-node. So reclaim on one node can still walk the whole
memcg queue and split underused THPs from other nodes in the same memcg.

But I think the new one can lose reclaim in the cgroup.memory=nokmem
case ...

With nokmem, the deferred shrinker can still run from memcg reclaim,
because it is SHRINKER_NONSLAB. But the list_lru is no longer per-memcg:

__list_lru_init() clears memcg_aware,

	if (mem_cgroup_kmem_disabled())
		memcg_aware = false;

so list_lru_from_memcg_idx() falls back to the shared node list:

static inline struct list_lru_one *
list_lru_from_memcg_idx(struct list_lru *lru, int nid, int idx)
{
	if (list_lru_memcg_aware(lru) && idx >= 0) {
[...]
	}
	return &lru->node[nid].lru;
}

That makes the shrinker bit unreliable. __list_lru_add() still sets the
bit on the memcg passed in, but only when the list goes from empty to
non-empty:

bool __list_lru_add(struct list_lru *lru, struct list_lru_one *l,
		    struct list_head *item, int nid,
		    struct mem_cgroup *memcg)
{
	if (list_empty(item)) {
[...]
		if (!l->nr_items++)
			set_shrinker_bit(memcg, nid, lru_shrinker_id(lru));
[...]
		return true;
	}
	return false;
}

If memcg A adds the first folio, A gets the bit. If memcg B later adds a
folio to the same shared list, B does not get a bit, because the list
was already non-empty.

So in the A-first/B-later case, reclaim from B may not call the deferred
shrinker at all. The shared list is scanned from memcg reclaim only if
reclaim runs from the memcg that has the bit, such as A here, or from
global reclaim :)

Anyway, only after the shared list is emptied does the next memcg to add
a folio get to be the one with the bit, IIUC :)

Hopefully I didn't miss somthing important ...

Cheers, Lance

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH] mm/compaction: cap compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX
patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch limits the value returned by compact_gap() to prevent over-reserving memory for compaction, which can lead to unnecessary reclaim and failed compaction suitability checks. The current implementation returns a gap that scales exponentially with order, but is bounded by COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX. By capping this value at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX, the patch aims to improve compaction success rates and reduce unnecessary reclaim on fragmented production hosts.

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX would lead to unnecessary reclamation due to genuine memory pressure, suggesting that processes may continuously allocate and reclaim without the gap

Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether the reduction in reclaim is due to increased allocation size or a shift to direct reclaim, requesting clarification on the actual cause.

Reviewer Vlastimil expressed skepticism about the patch's A/B test results, questioning why the free memory would suddenly stabilize and improve significantly after capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX.
sentiment_signals: raised a concern about potential performance impact
review_comments.raw_body:
But doesn't that mean there's genuine memory pressure? We're effectively
raising the high watermark by 4 MB, but if processes are continuously
allocating, we'd be reclaiming without the gap as well? Unless the workload
is sized to fit without the gap.

---

Did the extra reclaim just disappear because we allow the allocations to use
4MB more memory? Or it shifted to direct reclaim?

---

Weird that things would improve that much. I would expect the free memory
just to stabilize around the lower gap but then behave similarly. Are we
missing something here?

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged that order-9 THP failures were waking up kswapd and identified compact_gap() as a contributing factor, but did not indicate plans for further revision.

Author acknowledged that the patch's performance improvements are consistent across different kernel versions and builds, but did not address any specific technical concerns or provide a clear resolution to previous feedback.

Author addresses Vlastimil's concern about the impact of capping compact_gap() on order-9 THP allocations, explaining that it allows compaction to take over sooner and start working to produce large size pages needed for THP.

Author acknowledged that bursty network allocations can reserve many pageblocks as high atomic, which affects the patch's assumptions about THP-size pages becoming eligible for allocation.

Author is addressing concerns about over-reserving compact_gap() space, proposes to refactor patch to make it reclaim-focused and use a separate function for non-reclaim users.
sentiment_signals: did not commit to fixing it
author provided additional information but no direct response to prior feedback
review_comments.raw_body:
It wasn't actual pressure, but the repetitive order-9 THP failures that were
waking up kswapd. I should make this more clear in the changelog. After
looking into why so much reclaim was occurring though, the compact gap stood
out since it dictates the target amount to reclaim.

---

6.13. Additional benchmarks were done using a recent mm-new build as well,
and they showed similar reductions in reclaim.

---

Specifically in the order-9 case, the reclaim target goes from 1024 to 32.
What the data shows is that capping the gap allows compaction to take over
sooner and start working to produce large size pages needed for THP. Whereas
in the pre-patch state, trying to reclaim the full 2x THP delays compaction.

---

This patch was tested in isolation, but also occurring was the case where
bursty net allocations reserve many pageblocks as high atomic. So as
THP-size pages become eligible, their blocks are reserved before being
allocated as THP.

---

I'm thinking I could reframe this patch as reclaim-focused and use
min(2UL << order, COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX) as a reclaim-only target, while
either leaving the other non-reclaim users of this function alone or
using the 2x form you suggest above. i.e. I can split this function
into a separate reclaim_compact_gap() and use the originally proposed cap.
Thoughts?

review_comments.author: Vlastimil (SUSE)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer questioned whether the patch's logic for reclaiming memory still uses the high watermark plus compact_gap() threshold, instead of just reclaiming at least compact_gap() pages, and asked if kswapd's work indicates that memory pressure is present.

Reviewer noted that the patch does not address NUMA machines and suggested considering a recent fix (9c9828d3ead6) to prevent over-reclaim for __GFP_THISNODE THP allocations

Reviewer Vlastimil questioned whether the patch addresses a potential issue due to lack of hysteresis in compaction suitability checks, noting that high watermark + compact_gap() is required for termination but compaction can find out it's below this threshold due to concurrent allocations. They suggested examining compaction_ready(), which wants high wmark plus gap plus low-min difference.

Reviewer questioned whether capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX would improve compaction suitability checks, pointing out that it could potentially worsen the outcome of these checks.
sentiment_signals: asked for confirmation of mglru usage
review_comments.raw_body:
But the "amount to reclaim" is still defined as "reach high watermark +
compact_gap()" and not "reclaim at least compact_gap() pages" right? Or did
I miss something non-obvious.

So if kswapd did any work, it means the memory was consumed (i.e. there was
some memory pressure) and amount of free memory was below high watermark +
compact_gap()?

BTW, are you using mglru here? (probably not)
As that might be different and I'm not so familiar with it.

---

If it's a NUMA machine, we recently found an over-reclaim issue there fixed
by 9c9828d3ead6 ("mm, page_alloc, thp: prevent reclaim for __GFP_THISNODE
THP allocations")

---

So do I understand correctly we might have an issue due to lack of
hysteresis? We require reaching high watermark + compact_gap() to terminate
reclaim, but then compaction can find out we meanwhile dropped below that
(due to concurrent allocations) and it's not suitable again?

However the suitability checks e.g. compaction_zonelist_suitable() are using
min watermark, so that should provide the difference already.
Actually it's low watermark because of __compaction_suitable() adding an
extra low-min gap for costly orders. But still.

I did just notice compaction_ready() might be too strict. It wants
effectivly high wmark plus the gap plus the low-min difference. Is it
perhaps the underlying issue here?

---

Do I understand correctly you want to cap the reclaim target by
COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX but leave e.g. the compaction_suitable() usage as it is?
But wouldn't that mean we'll actually make changes of passing
compaction_suitable() worse?

review_comments.author: JP Kobryn (author)
review_comments.summary:
The author addressed a concern that compact_gap() is not actually used as the reclaim target in kswapd_shrink_node(), but rather as a threshold for abandoning high order reclaim, and explained how this affects the interaction between kswapd and kcompactd.

Author acknowledged that kswapd can be woken up on a high order failure despite lower order availability, suggesting compact_gap() still matters for higher orders.

Author acknowledged that their patch and another recent change are addressing different paths in memory compaction, confirmed that capping compact_gap() is still beneficial despite the other change.

The author addressed a concern that capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX would cause compaction_suitable() to remain false in fragmented environments, leading to kswapd continuing to reclaim in high order mode. The author explained that by capping the gap, compaction becomes suitable sooner and kswapd can fall back to order-0 balancing earlier, allowing kcompactd to be woken up for the original high order request.

Author acknowledges a concern about the impact of compact_gap() on compaction decisions, but suggests that addressing it in this patch would be a different direction from its original focus.

Author agrees that capping compact_gap() at COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX is a better approach than their previous attempt at finding middle ground and confirms that a 2x cap resulted in mixed results, including some cases where it was worse.
review_comments.raw_body:
Within kswapd_shrink_node(), sc->nr_to_reclaim is the sum of max(zone high
watermark or SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX) for each zone combined. The gap is not 
added to
that reclaim target though. It's used afterward as the threshold for 
abandoning
high order reclaim:

if (sc->order && sc->nr_reclaimed >= compact_gap(sc->order))
   sc->order = 0;

balance_pgdat() then returns sc->order and that becomes the kswapd 
reclaim_order
value, allowing this branch to be taken:

if (reclaim_order < alloc_order)
   goto kswapd_try_sleep;

Then in prepare_kswapd_sleep(), if pgdat_balanced() succeeds (at order-0),
kcompactd is woken up for the original alloc_order (order-9).

---

Hmm but kswapd can be woken up on a high order failure despite plenty of 
lower
order availability. That's really the case where compact_gap() matters for
higher orders. Unless by pressure you mean the high order pages were gone?

---

Thanks for pointing this out. I tested this on a recent mm-new built that
includes 9c9828d3ead6, and I found the compact_gap() change was still 
helpful.
My understanding is that 9c9828d3ead6 addresses direct reclaim for THP
allocations, while this patch affects the kswapd reclaim-compaction hand-off
path. The test runs still showed a benefit from capping the gap.

---

On an unpatched kernel in a fragmented environment, 
compaction_suitable() can
remain false because the effective threshold for costly orders is the low
watermark + the compact gap. Kswapd has to keep reclaiming in high order 
mode
as a result. By capping the gap at SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX, compaction becomes 
suitable
sooner and kswapd reaches the high order reclaim cutoff sooner. So with 
the patch,
kswapd is able to fall back to order-0 balancing earlier and wake up 
kcompactd
for the original high order request.

---

It's a good point. It does seem like that's worth looking into, and I'd be
happy to explore that separately. My thought at the moment though is that
changing compaction_ready() would be a different direction from the the 
original
focus of this patch, which started with the realization that the compaction
scanner working set is bounded by COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX. Since 
compact_gap() is
used in multiple reclaim and compaction decisions, including 
compaction_ready(),
fixing its definition seemed like the right first change if the gap 
itself is
oversized.

---

Good call. I was trying to find some middle ground, but I realize that the
change is better left unified.

Also, I tested a 2x COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX cap and I saw mixed results - either
similar to this patch or worse, with no improvements over the
COMPACT_CLUSTER_MAX cap.
